coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon nanodots from Bombax ceiba leaves for enhanced Tribo-corrosion resistance: An experimental and computational analysis

coredata.dc:description: This study presents a novel method for synthesizing carbon nanodots (BC-CNDs) from Bombax ceiba leaf extract via microwave assistance, yielding cost-effective, biodegradable, and water-soluble nanomaterials. Spectroscopic analysis, including FT-IR, UV–Vis, and fluorescence emission spectrophotometry, revealed the distinctive characteristics of BC-CNDs. Additionally, an anti-corrosive coating (BC-CNDs-C) was formulated by blending BC-CNDs with a plasticizer and binder. Quantitative evaluation of BC-CNDs-C showed an impressive inhibitory rate of 92.55% against corrosion. Electrochemical measurements indicated a notable increase in polarization resistance (Rp values: 1782.2 Ω cm2 for P1 to 4778.3 Ω cm2 for P4) and a decrease in corrosion current density (Icorr values: 0.065 μA/cm2 for P1 to 0.010 μA/cm2 for P4) with the incorporation of BC-CNDs, highlighting their effectiveness in enhancing corrosion resistance. Additionally, structural analyses via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) confirmed the reliability of BC-CNDs-C as a corrosion barrier. This research contributes to the development of sustainable materials for corrosion protection, addressing corrosion-related challenges in various industrial applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a novel method for synthesizing carbon nanodots (BC-CNDs) from Bombax ceiba leaf extract via microwave assistance, yielding cost-effective, biodegradable, and water-soluble nanomaterials. Spectroscopic analysis, including FT-IR, UV–Vis, and fluorescence emission spectrophotometry, revealed the distinctive characteristics of BC-CNDs. Additionally, an anti-corrosive coating (BC-CNDs-C) was formulated by blending BC-CNDs with a plasticizer and binder. Quantitative evaluation of BC-CNDs-C showed an impressive inhibitory rate of 92.55% against corrosion. Electrochemical measurements indicated a notable increase in polarization resistance (Rp values: 1782.2 Ω cm2 for P1 to 4778.3 Ω cm2 for P4) and a decrease in corrosion current density (Icorr values: 0.065 μA/cm2 for P1 to 0.010 μA/cm2 for P4) with the incorporation of BC-CNDs, highlighting their effectiveness in enhancing corrosion resistance. Additionally, structural analyses via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) confirmed the reliability of BC-CNDs-C as a corrosion barrier. This research contributes to the development of sustainable materials for corrosion protection, addressing corrosion-related challenges in various industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel's versatility makes it a preferred choice in numerous applications. For instance, in construction, mild steel is commonly used for fabricating steel frame buildings due to its structural strength and ability to withstand various loads. Specifically, mild steel exhibits a tensile strength of approximately 400–550 MPa and a yield strength of around 250–350 MPa, which contribute to its robust performance in structural applications. Additionally, mild steel has a ductility that allows it to be easily welded and shaped without losing its strength, with an elongation at break typically ranging from 20 to 25%. Its density, about 7.85 g/cm³, ensures a strong yet manageable weight for construction purposes. Moreover, mild steel's good balance of toughness and malleability makes it ideal for constructing beams, columns, and reinforcements in buildings, bridges, and other infrastructural projects. These properties, combined with its cost-effectiveness, make mild steel an essential material in the construction industry (Elmsellem et al., 2019; Basik et al., 2020; Ogunleye et al., 2018). However, in the oil and gas industries, a common practice involves the utilization of hydrochloric acid for essential tasks such as cleaning, pickling, oil well acidizing, and acid descaling (Fernandes et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2020). Unfortunately, this regular exposure of mild steel to the corrosive nature of hydrochloric acid precipitates corrosion, posing significant challenges to the longevity and safety of such structures and equipment. To mitigate the corrosive impact on mild steel, protective measures are imperative, often involving the application of coatings made from corrosion-resistant materials. Over time, numerous options for anti-corrosive coatings have been explored, encompassing both synthetic and environmentally friendly alternatives (Ogunleye et al., 2018; Chai et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018; Saxena et al., 2018). Among these, coatings derived from natural sources, particularly plant extracts, have garnered significant interest. These bio-based coatings offer several advantages, including non-toxicity, biodegradability, and notably, a heightened ability to resist corrosion effectively.

Carbon nanodots, also referred to as carbon quantum dots (CDs), constitute a fascinating category of carbon nanoparticles, typically smaller than 10 nm. They exhibit remarkable versatility and hold immense potential for various applications. The mechanism underlying the enhanced anti-corrosion properties of CDs (Guo et al., 2022), particularly when derived from plant materials, involves several key factors. CDs, characterized by their small size (typically smaller than 10 nm) and unique structure, possess oxygen and nitrogen atoms with lone pairs of electrons (Feng et al., 2019; Rivero et al., 2018). These functional groups contribute to their superior anti-corrosion properties. When applied to metal surfaces, CDs form robust covalent bonds due to their high-water solubility and the presence of functional groups. This bonding displaces water molecules and creates a protective barrier over the metal surface, effectively preventing corrosive agents from accessing the underlying material. The synthesis of CDs can be achieved through diverse approaches, including top-down and bottom-up strategies (Lisicki et al., 2021). Top-down methods involve techniques such as arc discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical processes, while bottom-up approaches encompass plasma treatments, chemical methodologies, hydrothermal and solvothermal treatments, as well as microwave-assisted synthesis. Notably, microwave-assisted synthesis has gained prominence due to its cost-effectiveness and eco-friendliness, driven by its capacity for rapid heating and the generation of low thermal gradients. This methodology holds substantial promise for efficiently producing materials that safeguard against corrosion, presenting a sustainable and effective solution for preserving mild steel in corrosive environments. Several articles (Basik et al., 2020; Cui et al., 2021; Cen et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021a, 2021b; Guo et al., 2023) reported the excellent corrosion protection efficiency of carbon dots integrated with the corrosion inhibitors for several metals and alloys.

In this study, we introduced an environmentally conscious method to synthesize carbon nanodots (BC-CNDs) derived from Bombax ceiba leaf extract, employing microwave-assisted synthesis. Bombax ceiba commonly referred to as the red silk-cotton tree or Indian cottonwood, is a prominent tropical species native to the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and parts of East Asia (Zhang et al., 2018). It is characterized by its imposing stature, reaching heights of up to 60 m, with a stout trunk and spreading branches forming a majestic crown. The tree is distinguished by its large, vibrant red flowers, which bloom before the emergence of new leaves, adding to its ornamental value. Beyond its aesthetic appeal, Bombax ceiba holds ecological significance, providing habitat and sustenance for diverse wildlife species, while its extensive root system helps prevent soil erosion along riverbanks and slopes (Saxena et al., 2018; Peme et al., 2015). Culturally, the tree is deeply rooted in tradition, often planted near temples and revered in Hindu mythology. Additionally, various parts of Bombax ceiba, including the bark, leaves, and flowers, are utilized in traditional medicine for their purported medicinal properties, addressing a range of ailments. Commercially, the lightweight and buoyant fibers surrounding the tree's seeds, known as kapok, find application in filling life jackets, mattresses, and insulation materials, while its timber is utilized in construction and furniture making.

Following, BC-CNDs were then integrated into a paint formulation designed for application in highly corrosive 1.0 M HCl solutions, serving as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel. The incorporation of BC-CNDs in the coatings offered several notable advantages, including enhanced protective properties such as high hardness, uniform dispersion of BC-CNDs, resistance to acidic environments, and resilience to UV radiation, ensuring prolonged corrosion protection for mild steel surfaces (Yuan et al., 2020; Ansari et al., 2020). Our evaluation of the inhibitory effect of these BC-CNDs-blended coatings encompassed a comprehensive range of electrochemical and non-electrochemical techniques, providing a thorough understanding of their corrosion prevention capabilities. Additionally, we conducted surface characterization analyses using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to visually assess the morphology of the mild steel specimens, offering valuable insights into the protective mechanisms of the BC-CNDs-blended coatings. Furthermore, supplementary analyses using atomic force microscopy (AFM) and contact angle techniques were employed to deepen our understanding of the surface properties and interactions between the BC-CNDs-blended coatings and the mild steel substrates. Our study represents a holistic approach to exploring the utilization of BC-CNDs as corrosion inhibitors in mild steel coatings, integrating eco-friendly synthesis methods with comprehensive protective properties assessment and surface characterization techniques to advance effective corrosion prevention strategies (Policarpi and Spinelli, 2020; Hossain et al., 2021). Fig. 1 highlights the presence of major components, mangiferin and isoquercetin, in Bombax ceiba leaf extract (Hassan et al., 2024; Bhosale et al., 2024; Huang et al., 2023; Alrabie et al., 2023).

The process began by gathering fresh leaves of Bombax ceiba from the Trichy district of Tamil Nadu, ensuring the selection of leaves at their peak freshness and potency. Phytochemical studies conducted on the leaf extract unveiled the presence of several major phytochemical components, including alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, and carbohydrates (Obot et al., 2019; Rahman et al., 2018). Specifically, the availability of these phytochemicals underscores the potential of Bombax ceiba leaf extract in areas such as corrosion prevention, paving the way for future investigations into its efficacy and practical applications in industrial settings.

In this procedure, fresh leaves from the Bombax ceiba plant were gathered and prepared for analysis through a specific treatment process. Initially, the freshly collected leaves underwent natural drying in the shade over three days to gently remove moisture while preserving their integrity (Aslam et al., 2021). Once sufficiently dried, the leaves were finely powdered using mechanical pulverization or smashing to facilitate subsequent extraction processes. To extract bioactive compounds and phytochemicals, 10 g of the powdered plant material were combined with 100 mL of double-distilled water, known for its high purity.

The mixture was then boiled to solubilize the compounds into the water, reducing the volume to 50 mL to concentrate the extract. After boiling, the extract was allowed to cool naturally before undergoing purification (Lv et al., 2019; Rbaa et al., 2021). To remove any residual plant material or solid impurities, the cooled extract was carefully filtered using Whatman filter paper. This filtration process ensured the final extract was free from particulate matter, resulting in a clear and purified aqueous extract of Bombax ceiba suitable for further analysis and experimentation.

For the synthesis of BC-CNDs (Bombax ceiba-derived carbon nanodots), we utilized a kitchenette-style microwave oven with an output power of 800 W in our experimental setup. Fig. 2 shows the whole procedure for the synthesis of BC-CNDs. The synthesis process began by placing 1 mL of ultra-pure water in a ceramic container, serving as the reaction medium, with Bombax ceiba aqueous extract as the primary carbon source. The earthenware containers containing the reaction medium were then positioned inside the microwave oven, set to their maximum power output of 800 W, and allowed to react for 15 min (Sarin et al., 2004; Journal, 2015). These specific time and power settings were chosen to ensure efficient and controlled synthesis of BC-CNDs.

After microwave-assisted synthesis, residual material containing the newly formed BC-CNDs was carefully scraped from the containers and liquefied by dissolving it in ultra-pure water. Subsequently, the mixture underwent centrifugation to further purify the sample and separate any dense particles. The solvent containing the carbon nanodots was then subjected to evaporation at a controlled temperature of 80 °C to obtain BC-CNDs in their highly luminescent and finely dispersed form (Olivares-xometl et al., 2017). The result of this evaporation process was the formation of extremely bright carbon nanodots, appearing as a black, fine dust-like material.

In this experiment, Oleic acid played the role of the plasticizer, contributing to the flexibility and durability of the paint formulation, while colophony, a rosin-type resin, served as the binder, ensuring adhesion of the paint to the substrate. The paint was meticulously prepared by combining all the ingredients using a magnetic stirrer to achieve an effective dispersion throughout the mixture (Guedes et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2002). To dissolve WW rosin and oleic acid, forming an integral part of the paint matrix, a high-speed disperser was utilized in a xylene/white spirit combination (1:1). Additionally, zinc oxide and calcium carbonate, serving as colorants and additives, were incorporated into the mixture and allowed to disperse for 24 h to ensure uniform distribution.

For the formulation of anticorrosion paints (Paints 2, 3, and 4), varying percentages of BC-CNDs were introduced into the matrix paint and allowed to disperse for 5 h. This step aimed to incorporate the corrosion-inhibiting properties of BC-CNDs into the paint matrix effectively. Subsequently, the paint was prepared for coating after the dispersion period. To assess the durability of the coatings, particularly their resistance to separation from the substrates, coated samples were subjected to a scratch test, wherein an “X" was scratched onto the surface. This evaluation method, based on ASTM D7187-15, provided insights into the adhesion and integrity of the paint coatings, crucial for their effectiveness in corrosion protection. Overall, the careful formulation and evaluation process ensured the development of durable and effective anticorrosion paint formulations incorporating BC-CNDs.

The mild steel coupons, measuring 1 × 4 × 0.1 cm having compositions: C-0.08, Mn-1.27, Si-0.01, P-0.02, and Fe-98.62%, underwent mechanical refinement to achieve a mirror finish before initiating the weight reduction process. Acetone was then employed to eliminate any strains present on the surface. Electrochemical tests utilized mild steel strips with a 1 cm2 exposed area embedded in Teflon. Before desiccation, the strips were meticulously cleaned with acetone and polished using abrasive sheets of grades 0/0, 2/0, 3/0, and 4/0 to ensure optimal adhesion between the primer and metallic substrate. The mild steel specimens were cleaned following the requirements outlined in ASTM G31. This ensured that the specimens were free from contaminants and ready for corrosion testing.

Subsequently, epoxy-based paint was applied to the specimens (Kuznetsov and Redkina, 2022). This study employed four distinct types of paint, with Paint 1 (lacking BC-CNDs) acting as the control. Additionally, Paint 2, Paint 3, and Paint 4 were utilized, with varying percentages of BC-CNDs added to each: 2%, 6%, and 10%, respectively. The experiment was conducted over 60 days to evaluate the corrosion protection performance of the coatings.

The investigation involved immersing pre-weighed specimens coated with various percentages of BC-CNDs on mild steel strips into blank HCl solution at room temperature for a duration of 60 days. Every 12 days, the plates underwent removal from the solution, followed by cleaning with HNO3 to eliminate corrosion products and contaminants. The reweighing of the plates enabled the calculation of weight loss, indicative of the erosion rate. Additionally, the erosion rate, degree of surface coverage, and efficacy of corrosion mitigation were assessed using specific formulas (Malathy et al., 2021; Saji, 2019). The erosion rate was determined by dividing the weight loss by the product of the immersion time and surface area, providing insight into the rate of coating material removal due to corrosion. Surface coverage was evaluated by comparing the coated surface area with the total surface area of the specimen, offering a measure of the proportion of the protected surface. Mitigation efficacy was assessed by comparing erosion rates of coated specimens with those of uncoated or differently coated specimens, indicating the relative effectiveness of the coatings in retarding corrosion using the ensuing formulas (1-3):
(1) CR (mpy) = (87.6 × ΔW)/(D × A × T)
ΔW = Weight loss in milligrams (mg),
D = Thickness (gm/cm3), A = area (cm2), and.

T = Immersion time (hours).
(2) I.E. (%) = (WP1-WPn/WP1) /100
WP1 = Weight loss of mild steel coated with P1

WPn = Weight loss of mild steel coated with P2, P3, P4
(3) θ = I.E./100

In the experimental procedure, mild steel strips coated with the developed paint were submerged in a pure acid solution, where potentiodynamic polarization tests, a vital electrochemical technique for assessing corrosion behavior, were conducted (Umoren et al., 2014). The primary objective was to gather specific electrochemical data, including corrosion potential, Tafel slopes, and decomposition current, at both the cathode and anode. These parameters yield valuable insights into how our paint-coated mild steel interacts with the corrosive environment, offering a comprehensive understanding of its corrosion resistance properties. To facilitate these tests, a three-electrode setup within the polarization cells, following the standard configuration in electrochemical studies, was employed. This setup comprised the working electrode (paint-coated mild steel strips), the reference electrode (providing a stable voltage reference point), and the counter electrode (maintaining charge neutrality in electrochemical reactions). The corrosion cell used for electrochemical measurements had a total volume of 500 mL. Such configuration allowed precise control and measurement of electrochemical parameters during the immersion process, enabling an assessment of the effectiveness of our anti-corrosion coatings and their potential for practical applications.
(4) (% I.E.) = ((Icorr)P1-(Icorr)Pn/(Icorr)Pn) × 100
(Icorr)P1 = Corrosion current in mild steel coated with P1
(Icorr)Pn = Corrosion current in mild steel coated with P2, P3, P4

Density functional theory (DFT)-based quantum chemical investigation is frequently used to analyze the fundamental relationship between metal surfaces and corrosion inhibitors. Owing to duration, expense, and ecosystem aspects, and the insufficiency of traditional methodologies, the utilization of computational methods such as DFT for investigations of metal corrosion inhibition has increased exponentially. This technique allows one to assess an organic compound's ability to reduce metal corrosion by evaluating its chemical reactivity. In this experiment, we have utilized the DFT approach, utilizing the GGA technique with BLYP functional to investigate electron-correlation encounters and a basis set comprised of DNP 3.5 to carry out theoretical calculations and calculate the EHOMO and ELUMO (highest occupied molecular orbital, lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) of the examining inhibitor in explaining their adsorption and corrosion protection efficiency. The chemical quantum investigation was performed by employing the Gaussian 09 W program. Several quantum chemical parameters like ELUMO, EHOMO, ionization potential (I), electronic affinity (A), global hardness (η), electonegativity (χ) and were calculated by using eqns. (5)–(9) to assess the overall reactivity of the examined extract.

The UV–visible and fluorescence spectra, depicted in Fig. 3 a and b, provided significant insights into the optical properties of the synthesized carbon dots (C-dots). In the UV–visible spectra, a discernible π-π∗ shift was observed, particularly evident at the peak around 205 nm, indicating the presence of CC bonds. Furthermore, another pronounced π-π∗ shift, characteristic of aromatic ring CC bonds, was evident near the peak at 230 nm. Moreover, the distinct peak at 290 nm highlighted the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of the C-dots, indicative of a CO bond's n-π∗ transition (Verma and Quraishi, 2022; Ramezanzadeh et al., 2018). The synthesized carbon dots (C-dots) likely contribute to this mechanism through their surface functional groups, such as carboxyl groups highlighted in the UV–visible spectra. These functional groups can interact with the metal surface, forming coordination complexes or chemisorption layers that impede the corrosion process. Additionally, the presence of π-π∗ shifts in the UV–visible spectra suggests the presence of CC bonds, which may enhance the adsorption of C-dots onto the metal surface through π-π interactions. This adsorption can further strengthen the protective barrier and inhibit the penetration of corrosive species (Sharma et al., 2023; Savaş Kaya Hassane Lgaz and Ben Ahmed, 2023; Assad et al., 2023).

Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra indicate the photoluminescent properties of the C-dots, which could play a role in corrosion inhibition. The interaction between the emitted photons and the metal surface may lead to localized energy transfer processes, altering the electrochemical reactions occurring at the metal-electrolyte interface and mitigating corrosion. To explore the photoluminescence characteristics of the newly synthesized carbon dots, a fluorescence spectrometer was employed (Goyal et al., 2018). A sample of the C-dots was placed within a cuvette and subjected to analysis using a fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The results revealed a clear and discernible fluorescence emission, emphasizing the photoluminescent property of the C-dots. To further elucidate the photoluminescent behavior, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected at various excitation wavelengths, including 240, 260, 280, 300, 320, and 340 nm, selected based on the absorption characteristics of the C-dots. Remarkably, as the excitation wavelength increased, the PL intensity consistently rose and shifted to lower wavelengths. This intriguing behavior suggests that the strong emission observed in the fluorescence spectra may be attributed to specific functional groups on the C-dots' surface, which act as emissive traps during electronic transitions.

FT-IR analysis was conducted to gain structural insights into the surface functionalization of C-dots. As depicted in Fig. 4 a and b, abundant hydrophilic groups were observed, characterized by spectrum stretching frequencies outlined in Table 1 . Table 1 presents the FT-IR data comparing the spectral frequencies and peak assignments of Bombax ceiba (BC) leaf extract and Bombax ceiba-derived carbon nanodots (BC-CNDs). The observed IR frequencies are listed alongside their respective peak assignments for both the plant extract and the synthesized C-dots. For the O–H stretching vibration, the frequencies observed were 3420 cm−1 for the plant extract and 3399 cm−1 for BC-CNDs. This vibration corresponds to hydroxyl (OH) functional groups, indicating the presence of hydrophilic moieties in both the extract and the C-dots.

The H–C–H asymmetrical stretching vibration, indicative of aliphatic hydrocarbons, was observed at frequencies of 2960 cm−1 for the plant extract and 2977 cm−1 for BC-CNDs. This suggests the presence of similar aliphatic hydrocarbon structures in both samples (Abd El-Lateef et al., 2015; Abod et al., 2019). The CO stretching vibration, associated with carbonyl (CO) functional groups, was observed at frequencies of 1638 cm−1 for the plant extract and 1645 cm−1 for BC-CNDs. This indicates the presence of carbonyl groups in both samples, possibly originating from organic acids or esters. The C–O–C stretching vibration, characteristic of ether (C–O–C) linkages, was observed at frequencies of 1260 cm−1 for the plant extract and 1279 cm−1 for BC-CNDs. This suggests the presence of ether linkages in both samples, which may be attributed to polysaccharides or other organic compounds.

Lastly, the C–H “oop” bending vibration was observed at frequencies of 663 cm−1 for the plant extract and 681 cm−1 for BC-CNDs. This vibration corresponds to out-of-plane bending of carbon-hydrogen (C–H) bonds, indicating the presence of aromatic and aliphatic hydrocarbons in both samples. Moreover, the functionalized surface of C-dots, particularly the hetero groups, holds potential for interaction with the metal surface. Previous research suggests that the interaction of hetero atoms with the metal surface leads to the formation of an insoluble protective complex. Thus, it is presumed that C-dots derived from Bombax ceiba leaf extract possess corrosion inhibition activity on mild steel surfaces. The phytochemical study of desiccated leaf extracts of the plant resulted in the isolation of two active components: Mangiferin and isoquercetin (Fig. 1). These compounds have been previously recognized for their antioxidant and metal chelating properties, suggesting their potential role in corrosion inhibition. Mangiferin, a xanthone glycoside, is known for its strong antioxidant activity and ability to chelate metal ions, while isoquercetin, a flavonoid, also exhibits potent antioxidant properties. The presence of these bioactive compounds in the leaf extract further supports the hypothesis that the synthesized C-dots derived from Bombax ceiba leaves could effectively inhibit corrosion on mild steel surfaces.

Table 2 provides a comprehensive view of the weight loss measurements obtained after subjecting mild steel specimens to a 60-day immersion in different paint coatings, specifically Paints 1, 2, 3, and 4. Notably, an interesting trend emerges from the data: there is a clear correlation between the number of carbon nanodots (CNDs) incorporated into the paint and the reduction in specimen weight loss (Benghalia et al., 2018; Ameh, 2018). This relationship underscores the effectiveness of CNDs as corrosion inhibitors, as indicated by the FT-IR results, which serve as an indicator of the active ingredients present in the paint formulation. After the 60-day immersion period in the acidic solution, the weight losses of the specimens and their corresponding corrosion rates are summarized in Table 2 for reference and comparison. This comparison is particularly insightful when examining Paint 4, which contains the highest concentration of BC-CNDs, in contrast to Paint 1, which lacks any inhibitor. In this context, it is evident that Paint 4 exhibited the lowest weight reduction among all the paints, with a decrease of approximately 0.70%, as opposed to Paint 1, which experienced a weight loss of 2.20%.

Furthermore, by increasing the concentration of BC-CNDs in the coatings from Paint 1 to Paint 4, a notable reduction in the corrosion rate is observed. This reduction is quantified by the corrosion rate, which decreases from 0.005150 mm/year for Paint 1 to 0.000967 mm/year for Paint 4. These findings demonstrate the promising potential of BC-CNDs as highly effective corrosion inhibitors, highlighting their ability to significantly mitigate the corrosion of mild steel when integrated into paint coatings. This reduction in corrosion rate is a testament to the anti-corrosion properties of BC-CNDs and their practical utility in enhancing the longevity and durability of metal structures in corrosive environments.

The adsorption isotherm analysis serves as a valuable tool in unraveling the intricate mechanism underlying the interaction between BC-CNDs and the surfaces of mild steel. Illustrated in the accompanying figure is a linear plot, revealing a noteworthy coefficient of determination (R2) value of 0.9863. This high R2 value signifies a robust correlation between the experimental data points and the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, a widely acknowledged model for describing adsorption behavior with remarkable accuracy (Elgendy et al., 2019). The Langmuir adsorption isotherm holds particular relevance in this context due to its ability to provide a comprehensive framework for understanding the adsorption process of BC-CNDs onto mild steel surfaces. According to this model, adsorption occurs in a monolayer arrangement, wherein BC-CNDs adhere to the metal surface in a highly organized and uniform manner. This monolayer formation is indicative of a specific and controlled adsorption mechanism, shedding light on the precise nature of the interaction.

The close alignment observed between the experimental data and the Langmuir adsorption isotherm (Fig. 5 ) underscores the precision and reliability of the adsorption process involving BC-CNDs within the matrix paint. This alignment not only validates the applicability of the Langmuir model to describe the adsorption phenomenon but also suggests that the adsorption of BC-CNDs onto mild steel surfaces follows a well-defined and predictable pattern. Such findings hold significant implications for optimizing the application of BC-CNDs in corrosion protection and other surface modification processes. By gaining insights into the intricacies of the adsorption mechanism, it becomes possible to tailor the application of BC-CNDs for enhanced performance and efficacy in various practical scenarios, ultimately contributing to advancements in corrosion prevention strategies and surface engineering technologies.

The potential vs. time plot (Fig. 6 ) illustrates the electrochemical behavior of two samples—one without a coating (Blank) and one coated with paint—likely during a corrosion test. The blank sample, represented by the black curve, maintains a fairly stable potential around −0.472 V throughout the test, indicating a relatively steady corrosion process or surface reactivity. This consistency suggests that no significant barrier to corrosion exists, allowing the surface to corrode at a fairly constant rate. In contrast, the paint-coated sample, shown by the red curve, starts with a more negative potential at around −0.484 V. This initially lower potential implies that the paint coating may be reducing the corrosion rate or preventing the direct exposure of the metal to corrosive agents. Over time, the potential stabilizes slightly lower at around −0.488 V. This stabilization could indicate that the paint provides some degree of protection by forming a physical barrier that slows down the corrosion process. The difference in potential between the two samples suggests that the paint coating is effective in enhancing corrosion resistance. However, the fact that the potential of the painted sample still changes over time might suggest that the coating is not fully impermeable, allowing some corrosive interaction to occur, though at a reduced rate.

In light of these findings, the measured corrosion protection efficiency value was determined to be an impressive 92.5%. This value highlights the remarkable effectiveness of BC-CNDs in inhibiting corrosion and protecting mild steel specimens. The increase in polarization resistance and the reduction in capacitance of the double layer together signify the positive influence of BC-CNDs in forming protective barriers against corrosion. These results emphasize the potential of BC-CNDs as highly efficient corrosion inhibitors in practical applications, particularly in the development of anti-corrosion coatings for various industries.

Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) analysis was performed to study the corrosion phenomena on the specimen's surface. The SEM examination of specimens after 60 days of immersion in the HCl solution is depicted in Fig. 9 a, b, and 9c. Additionally, corrosion resulting from substrate exposure during electrochemical testing was observed. Upon comparing SEM images at the same magnification, it was evident that the surface appeared rough and severely damaged, as depicted in Fig. 9, which illustrates the surface of the specimen coated with P1 (Fouda et al., 2019; Fekkar et al., 2020). Conversely, with the addition of a high concentration of BC-CNDs, the surface exhibited more uniform deposits and appeared smoother. Therefore, it can be inferred that the high concentration of BC-CNDs in the paint matrix, as observed in P4, contributed to the inhibitive action by forming a protective layer on the substrate surface.

The two-dimensional and three-dimensional pictures of the exterior of mild steel dipped in HCl solution are exhibited in (Fig. 10 a). It has a permeable structure with large cavernous cavities. The exterior of the mild steel coupons was found to be covered with a defensive insoluble film (Fig. 10b and c). The insoluble film formed a barrier and kept the strips from deteriorating. Table 5 depicts the AFM data. The roughness value of refined steel is 121.18. The mean roughness Sa of the P1 metal surface dipped in blank HCl was determined to be 786.27 nm. However, the mean roughness of mild steel dipped in HCl with paint 4 varied from 203.55 nm to 786.27 nm when compared to blank paint 1 (Ramezanzadeh et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021). The decrease in mean roughness value results from CND adsorption with the coating on the mild steel outer area, which reduces the degree of deterioration.

DFT has become one of the most influential and remarkable computational techniques for evaluating efficient corrosion inhibitors. One of the most significant advantages of DFT and similar computational methods is their ability to theoretically analyze the corrosion inhibition capability and other chemical reactivity parameters of chemical entities before complex and hazardous chemical synthesis. Due to considerations of time, cost, and environmental impact, as well as the limitations of traditional methodologies, the use of computational methods like DFT in the investigation of metal corrosion inhibition has grown exponentially. This technique allows for the assessment of an organic compound's ability to reduce metal corrosion by evaluating its chemical reactivity. In our study, we employed the DFT approach using the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) technique with the BLYP functional to investigate electron-correlation encounters. We utilized a basis set comprised of DNP 3.5 for theoretical calculations and to determine the EHOMO and ELUMO of the inhibitor being examined, which are critical for explaining their adsorption and corrosion protection efficiency. The DMol3 tool in the Materials Studio version 6.0 package was used along with the COSMOS solvation model (H2O). Additionally, quantum chemical investigations were performed using Gaussian 09 W software, where parameters such as EHOMO, ELUMO, ionization potential (I), electron affinity (A), global hardness (η), and electronegativity (χ) were calculated using equations (7)–(11) to assess the overall reactivity of the examined extract.

Fig. 11 provides detailed information on the optimized geometries and electronic properties of Mangiferin and Isoquercetin molecules. By analyzing this figure, researchers can gain valuable insights into the spatial distribution of the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) within these compounds. Notably, both HOMO and LUMO orbitals are distributed relatively evenly across the entire molecular structures of Coumarin, Flavones, and Phenolic acid. This uniform distribution indicates a high degree of charge sharing within the molecules, suggesting that electrons are not confined to specific regions but are shared across multiple atoms. This phenomenon is particularly pronounced due to the presence of heteroatom moieties and aromatic ring configurations, which introduce additional electronic states and stabilize delocalized electrons through resonance, respectively. Consequently, Mangiferin and Isoquercetin exhibit substantial charge distribution, which is crucial for understanding their electronic properties and reactivity in various chemical environments.

Fig. 12 presents Electrostatic Potential Surface (ESP) maps, providing an intuitive method for quantifying polarity, which is crucial for evaluating the corrosion process. These maps show regions of negative electrostatic potential (shaded in red) that are susceptible to nucleophilic attacks and regions of positive electrostatic potential (shaded in blue) that are vulnerable to electrophilic attacks. Typically, the red areas are concentrated around heteroatoms, such as oxygen, which tend to form covalent bonds with metal atoms, as illustrated in Fig. 12. Conversely, positive potential sites are often located near carbon and hydrogen atoms, making them ideal targets for electrophilic attack. These insights reveal that molecules like Mangiferin and Isoquercetin exhibit exceptional anti-corrosive properties due to their effective interaction with metal surfaces, forming protective layers that shield against corrosive agents. The presence of functional groups, such as phenolic hydroxyl groups and conjugated double bonds, enhances their corrosion inhibition capabilities by facilitating adsorption onto metal surfaces and promoting the formation of stable passivation layers. Consequently, these molecules are promising candidates for corrosion inhibition strategies in various industrial applications, owing to their robust anti-corrosive efficacy and compatibility with metal substrates.

Table 6 outlines the pertinent electronic parameters for Mangiferin and Isoquercetin, evaluated in the aqueous phase using the DNP 3.5 basis set and the GGA/BLYP technique. These parameters include the EHOMO, ELUMO, ΔE, I, A, χ, η, ΔN and σ. Mangiferin, with an EHOMO of −0.26829 eV and an ELUMO of −0.1957 eV, results in an energy gap (ΔE) of 0.07259 eV. Isoquercetin, on the other hand, exhibits an EHOMO of −0.23461 eV and an ELUMO of −0.08949 eV, resulting in a larger energy gap of 0.14512 eV. The smaller energy gap of Mangiferin suggests higher reactivity, making it more effective in interacting with metal surfaces to inhibit corrosion. This reactivity is critical as it facilitates the formation of a stable protective layer on the metal surface, reducing the metal's susceptibility to corrosion. I and A further delineate the molecules' ability to donate and accept electrons. Mangiferin, with an ionization potential of 0.26829 eV and an electron affinity of 0.1957 eV, demonstrates a robust ability to participate in electron transfer processes. This dual capability enhances its interaction with the metal surface, forming covalent bonds that contribute to corrosion inhibition. Isoquercetin's lower values (I = 0.23461 eV, A = 0.08949 eV) indicate a comparatively reduced tendency for electron exchange, yet it remains significant for corrosion inhibition. χ and η provide additional insights into the molecular behavior of these inhibitors. Mangiferin has an electronegativity of 0.036295 eV and a global hardness of 0.231995 eV, indicating a balanced tendency to attract electrons while resisting deformation or polarization. Isoquercetin, with higher electronegativity (0.07256 eV) and lower hardness (0.16205 eV), suggests a stronger electron attraction and greater adaptability. The adaptability of Isoquercetin, reflected in its higher softness (σ = 6.17093), allows it to conform more effectively to the metal surface, enhancing its protective properties. Mangiferin, with a softness of 4.31043, while less adaptable, maintains significant stability, crucial for forming durable protective layers (Al-Adilee et al., 2024).

ΔN highlights the interaction strength between the inhibitor and the metal surface. Isoquercetin shows a higher ΔN value of 0.178060 e, compared to Mangiferin's 0.087797 e, indicating that Isoquercetin transfers more electrons to the metal surface. This higher electron transfer rate suggests stronger bonding and effective inhibition by creating a more substantial protective barrier. In conclusion, both Mangiferin and Isoquercetin exhibit properties that make them effective corrosion inhibitors. Mangiferin's higher reactivity, ionization potential, and electron affinity suggest a strong capability to form stable, protective layers on metal surfaces. Isoquercetin's greater electron transfer and softness indicate a strong adaptability and bonding capacity. Together, these attributes underscore the potential of these molecules to serve as robust corrosion inhibitors in various industrial applications, offering effective protection and enhancing the longevity of metal substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of corrosion inhibition by BC-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion inhibition mechanism of BC-CNDs derived from Bombax ceiba leaf extract is a multi-faceted process with several key aspects contributing to their effectiveness in mitigating corrosion. At its core, the mechanism involves the interaction between BC-CNDs and the metal surface immersed in a corrosive environment, such as an HCl solution. First and foremost, BC-CNDs possess functional groups on their surface, including oxygen-containing moieties like Mangiferin and Isoquercetin (Ebenso et al., 2008; Zaher et al., 2022). These functional groups have the essential property of lone pairs of electrons, making them capable of adsorbing onto the metal surface. This initial adsorption step is fundamental to the subsequent corrosion inhibition process. Upon adsorption, BC-CNDs establish a protective layer or barrier on the metal surface. This barrier physically separates the metal from the corrosive ions present in the solution. Essentially, BC-CNDs act as a shield, preventing direct contact between the metal and the corrosive agents. This shielding effect is akin to a defensive armor that obstructs the corrosion process from progressing unchecked. While our study demonstrates the significant corrosion resistance offered by BC-CNDs, the potential for galvanic corrosion must also be considered in practical applications involving dissimilar metals. Previous research by S. Saji et al. (Saji, 2021) has shown that the use of nanomaterials in coatings can influence the occurrence of galvanic corrosion, which is an important factor to investigate for long-term performance in industrial environments.

Furthermore, BC-CNDs may play a role in promoting the passivation of the metal surface. Passivation involves the formation of a thin, stable, and protective oxide layer on the metal's surface. This oxide layer serves as an additional barrier against further corrosion by limiting the diffusion of corrosive ions and inhibiting electrochemical reactions. BC-CNDs' presence can facilitate this passivation process, enhancing the overall protection of the metal. Additionally, the functional groups on BC-CNDs have the capacity to participate in electron transfer reactions. This electron transfer can interfere with the electrochemical reactions responsible for corrosion. By affecting the anodic and cathodic processes involved in metal dissolution, BC-CNDs slow down the corrosion rate, further bolstering their inhibitory effects. Moreover, BC-CNDs have the potential to modify various surface characteristics of the metal. These modifications can encompass changes in surface charge, wettability, and roughness. Such alterations can have a significant impact on the corrosion process, making it less favorable for corrosive agents to attack the metal. Furthermore, BC-CNDs may possess a self-healing capability. In cases where the protective layer is damaged or compromised, BC-CNDs can redistribute and reform the affected area, restoring the integrity of the protective barrier. This self-healing property ensures continued corrosion protection over time.

Table 7 presents a comparative evaluation of various corrosion inhibitors that function as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in harsh settings. It highlights the special qualities of BC-CNDs, exhibiting a remarkable maximal corrosion protection efficacy of 89.36 %. This finding demonstrates BC-CNDs superior performance over other corrosion inhibitors.

According to the findings, BC-CNDs coating is an effective coating of mild steel corrosion in HCl. Some of the potent attained results from sophisticated investigations are as follows.
•
The incorporation of BC-CNDs into the coating proved to be highly effective in mitigating mild steel corrosion in HCl revealing an impressive 92.5% efficacy according to the EIS study.
•
The polarization resistance increased with the presence of BC-CNDs, indicating enhanced resistance to corrosion. For example, Rp values rose from 1782.2 Ω cm2 (for P1) to 4778.3 Ω cm2 (for P4), demonstrating the effectiveness of BC-CNDs in forming a protective barrier.
•
The corrosion current density provides insight into the rate of corrosion. For example, in the presence of BC-CNDs, Icorr decreased from an initial value of 0.065 μA/cm2 (for P1) to as low as 0.010 μA/cm2 (for P4). This significant reduction highlights the ability of BC-CNDs to slow down the corrosion process.
•
Among the tested paints, P4, containing the highest percentage of BC-CNDs, emerged as the most efficient in inhibiting corrosion. This outcome was attributed to the increasing concentration of BC-CNDs and their ability to form a protective barrier on the surface, effectively resisting corrosion. It is worth noting that inhomogeneity in the coating samples influenced this outcome.
•
FTIR analysis provided insights into the compounds present in BC-CNDs, indicating the formation of a corrosion-inhibitive layer. Additionally, it revealed the potential for the inhibitor to adsorb onto the metal surface through the oxygen atom of key constituents, such as Mangiferin and Isoquercetin, found in BC-CNDs.
•
Morphological observations of the specimen surfaces revealed striking differences. In the absence of BC-CNDs, the surface exhibited roughness and visible damage. However, in the presence of BC-CNDs, the surface became smoother and more uniform, suggesting the protective nature of BC-CNDs in preserving the integrity of the mild steel surface.
